patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old anxious but clear-speaking call-center team lead. Start by saying: “My feet keep tripping me up, and my ankles feel weak.” Early in the visit, volunteer: you catch your toes and stumble on uneven sidewalks, your ankles feel weak and you’ve rolled them, and your arches have gotten higher so shoes don’t fit. If asked about progression, say it’s been slowly worsening since teens/early adulthood. If asked, add: lower legs look skinnier below the knees, hands tire with buttons/jars, intermittent numbness/tingling in feet, ankle reflexes “hard to find,” and relatives with “weak ankles”/braces.

Do not volunteer at first: reduced sensation to cuts/blisters, avoiding stairs/balance issues, curling toes and sore knuckles, night calf cramps, and you lift knees higher to avoid toe drag—share only if specifically asked about falls, sensation, foot shape, cramps, or gait.

Be embarrassed about public falls; minimize unless asked directly. If asked about alcohol, downplay weekend increase and request it not be documented. Mention misconceptions: pinched back nerve, diabetes worry, and “out of shape.” If clinician shows empathy, relax, thank them, and share more honestly, especially about safety, costs, work, and genetic-testing worries.

Electromyography (EMG).value: Chronic denervation with reinnervation changes in distal lower-extremity muscles; no active myositis pattern

medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg every 6 hours as needed for musculoskeletal pain
comorbidities: Chronic ankle instability with recurrent sprains
confounders: Family history of type 2 diabetes (raises concern for diabetic neuropathy despite normal glycemic testing)
History of recurrent ankle sprains and prior ankle fracture (can mimic/compound gait instability and distal weakness)
Intermittent heavy alcohol use on weekends (can contribute to peripheral neuropathy symptoms)
Longstanding use of tight, narrow footwear for work (worsens foot pain/calluses and may be misattributed to orthopedic causes)

required_evidence: Length-dependent distal weakness and atrophy
Distal sensory loss with reduced/absent reflexes
Slowly progressive course with foot deformities (pes cavus/hammer toes) or gait difficulty
Family history or prior genetic/EMG evidence consistent with hereditary neuropathy
Exclusion of common acquired neuropathy causes with available labs (diabetes, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, paraproteinemia)

medications: acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN pain (max 3 g/day)
gabapentin 300 mg PO qHS, increase to 300 mg PO TID as tolerated for neuropathic pain
non_pharmacological: Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance and gait training
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFO) for foot drop and gait stability
Occupational therapy for hand weakness and adaptive devices
Podiatry/orthopedics for pes cavus/hammer toes; consider surgical evaluation if severe deformity
Foot care and daily skin checks; fall-risk assessment and home safety modifications
Genetic counseling; consider genetic testing if not previously done
Avoid neurotoxic medications (e.g., vincristine, amiodarone, nitrofurantoin, metronidazole, linezolid, excessive alcohol)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to assess pain control, function, and therapy/orthotics progress; sooner for worsening weakness or falls

Start with: “My hands have been shaking and I can’t stop feeling this weird tingling in my fingers.” Volunteer early: you’ve been irritable/on edge, forgetful with poor focus at work, and having on-and-off headaches for weeks. Do not volunteer unless specifically asked about exposures: frequent tuna/swordfish meals, home soldering/heating metal with poor ventilation, darker urine/less urination, increased thirst (you attribute to diabetes), and dropping things/clumsiness. Start with: “I get out of breath really easily now, even just walking to the bathroom.” If asked open-endedly, volunteer: breathless with small tasks, wiped out, occasional racing heart, and a couple lightheaded episodes.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark requires the agent to correctly identify and record idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension as the primary diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (not generic pulmonary hypertension) and indicates it is a pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension entity.

Communicates key typical symptoms (e.g., exertional dyspnea, fatigue, chest discomfort, syncope/presyncope) and acknowledges that symptoms can be nonspecific early.
Mentions common supportive findings that raise suspicion (e.g., loud P2, RV heave, peripheral edema in later disease) without inventing results not provided.

Notes that confirmation/classification requires appropriate evaluation (e.g., echocardiography for screening and right heart catheterization for definitive hemodynamics) and avoids claiming a diagnosis is confirmed by echo alone.

Explains treatment at a high level, explicitly linking idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension management to vasodilators/PAH-targeted therapies, without prescribing (L1) or giving unsafe specific dosing.
Includes a safety-oriented statement that any future prescribing would require allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check before initiating therapy.

Avoids contraindicated or misleading advice (e.g., does not recommend routine calcium channel blockers without vasoreactivity testing; does not conflate with COPD/left-heart disease pulmonary hypertension).
Uses clear uncertainty language if data are incomplete and frames IPAH as a serious condition requiring specialist-led longitudinal care and monitoring.
